skill.name: multi-panel-figure-assembler
skill.description: Assemble 6 sub-figures (A-F) into a high-resolution composite figure with consistent labels, padding, and publication-ready DPI.
skill.summary: Automates the assembly of 6 image panels into a publication-ready composite figure with consistent styling and configurable layout.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 73 static analysis findings are false positives. Weak cryptographic algorithm flags are pattern-matching errors on non-cryptographic documentation text. Backtick execution flags correspond to markdown code blocks showing legitimate command-line usage. Path traversal flags document the skill's built-in security protection. No evidence of malicious intent, data exfiltration, credential access, or obfuscated code. The skill is a well-structured scientific figure assembly tool with proper input validation and scope enforcement.

low_findings.title: Markdown code blocks flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged backtick-delimited code blocks in SKILL.md as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are markdown documentation showing legitimate python command-line usage examples (e.g., python scripts/main.py --input ...). Not actual shell injection.

low_findings.title: Path traversal string in security documentation
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged ../ in SKILL.md line 86. This text documents the skill's built-in path traversal protection feature: 'If any input file path contains ../ or points outside the workspace, reject with a path traversal warning.' Not a vulnerability - it is a security control.

low_findings.title: GitHub URL in documentation reference
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged the hardcoded GitHub URL at SKILL.md line 7. This is a legitimate reference to the source repository for attribution. Not a data exfiltration endpoint.

content.value_statement: Creating publication-quality composite figures from individual plot panels is time-consuming and error-prone when done manually. This skill automates the assembly of 6 sub-figures into a high-resolution composite with consistent labels, padding, and DPI settings.
actual_capabilities: Assembles exactly 6 image panels into a 2x3 or 3x2 grid composite figure
Applies consistent panel labels (A-F) with configurable font, size, and position
Sets output DPI from 72 to 1200+ for publication-ready resolution
Adds customizable padding and borders between and around panels
Supports multiple input formats including PNG, JPG, TIFF, and BMP
Automatically resizes panels to uniform dimensions while preserving aspect ratio
limitations: Only supports exactly 6 panels - cannot handle 2, 4, 8, or 9-panel layouts
Cannot generate or edit image content within individual panels
Requires Python with Pillow and NumPy installed in the execution environment
Font rendering depends on system-available fonts

use_cases.title: Journal submission figure assembly
use_cases.description: A researcher has 6 individual plot panels from separate analysis scripts and needs them combined into a single composite figure meeting journal DPI and formatting requirements.
use_cases.target_user: Academic researcher preparing manuscripts for peer-reviewed journals

use_cases.title: Lab-wide figure standardization
use_cases.description: A lab manager wants all team members to produce consistently formatted figures with the same label style, padding, and resolution for lab presentations and publications.
use_cases.target_user: Laboratory manager or principal investigator standardizing output

use_cases.title: Reproducible figure generation
use_cases.description: An author needs to regenerate a set of composite figures from updated source images while maintaining exact layout and styling for reproducibility in version-controlled research.
use_cases.target_user: Computational researcher focused on reproducible workflows

prompt_templates.title: Basic figure assembly
prompt_templates.prompt: I have 6 image files for a composite figure. Assemble them into a 2x3 grid using default settings. My input files are panel_A.png through panel_F.png and I want the output saved as composite_figure.png.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user assembling a standard 6-panel figure with default parameters

prompt_templates.title: Custom layout and resolution
prompt_templates.prompt: Assemble these 6 figure panels into a 3x2 layout at 600 DPI with larger labels. Input files are figure_1.png through figure_6.png. Output should be manuscript_figure.tiff. Use label size 32 and padding of 15 pixels.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needing higher resolution and non-default grid arrangement

prompt_templates.title: Custom styling for publication
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a composite figure from these 6 panels with custom styling. Use a 2x3 grid, white background with 4-pixel black borders, label position bottomright, label color black on white background. Output at 300 DPI as PNG. Files are data_A.png through data_F.png.
prompt_templates.scenario: User customizing borders, label position, and color for specific journal guidelines

prompt_templates.title: Error recovery and fallback handling
prompt_templates.prompt: I tried to assemble 4 panels into a figure but got an error. My input files are result_1.png, result_2.png, result_3.png, and result_4.png. Use the fallback template to explain what went wrong and guide me to the correct input format.
prompt_templates.scenario: User encountering the 6-panel constraint and needing guidance

output_examples.input: 6 individual plot panels (A-F) in PNG format, each 800x600 pixels, requested in a 2x3 grid at 300 DPI with Arial labels at size 24
output_examples.output: A single 2436x1824 pixel composite PNG image arranged in a 2x3 grid. Each panel bordered in black with white background. Panel labels A through F appear at the top-left corner of each panel in Arial font. Saved at 300 DPI, suitable for journal submission.

output_examples.input: 6 microscopy image panels in TIFF format at various resolutions, requested in a 3x2 layout at 600 DPI with 20px padding
output_examples.output: A single composite TIFF image with all panels automatically resized to the largest dimensions while preserving aspect ratio. Panels arranged in 3 rows by 2 columns with 20-pixel white padding between them. Labels A-F appear at the top-left in the specified font. Output at 600 DPI for high-resolution printing.

best_practices: Use input images with similar aspect ratios for the most consistent composite output
Specify the output DPI to match your target journal or conference requirements before assembly
Verify that all 6 source files exist and are accessible before starting the assembly process
anti_patterns: Requesting assembly of more or fewer than 6 panels - the skill strictly enforces a 6-panel constraint
Asking the skill to generate plot data or edit image content within panels - this skill only arranges existing images
Providing input paths with ../ sequences - the skill rejects path traversal attempts for security

faq.question: How many panels can this skill assemble?
faq.answer: Exactly 6 panels labeled A through F. It supports 2x3 and 3x2 grid layouts but cannot handle other panel counts.

faq.question: What image formats are supported?
faq.answer: Input formats include PNG, JPG, JPEG, BMP, TIFF, and GIF. Output formats are PNG, JPG, and TIFF.

faq.question: What DPI values can I use?
faq.answer: Any positive integer DPI value. The default is 300 DPI. Common publication values are 300 for journals and 600 for high-resolution printing.

faq.question: What happens if my input images have different sizes?
faq.answer: All panels are automatically resized to match the largest width and height while preserving aspect ratio, using high-quality Lanczos resampling.

faq.question: Can I use custom panel labels instead of A-F?
faq.answer: Yes. You can provide custom labels when calling the assembler programmatically. Custom labels can be any text strings such as Fig 1 through Fig 6.

faq.question: What if a panel image has transparency?
faq.answer: Transparent areas are filled with the background color (default white). PNG output preserves transparency if any input images have alpha channels.
